male-55-worker.name: Robert Garcia
male-55-worker.chiefComplaint: Found collapsed at outdoor job site
male-55-worker.variantDescription: Exertional heat stroke in construction worker

missedConsequences.Heat Stroke: Every minute of delay in cooling increases mortality. Core temp >106°F for >30 min causes multi-organ failure, rhabdomyolysis, DIC, and death.
missedConsequences.Hypoglycemia: Unrecognized hypoglycemia leads to permanent brain injury.
missedConsequences.Cardiac Arrhythmia: Undiagnosed arrhythmia leads to cardiac arrest.

commonPitfalls: Waiting for labs before starting cooling - COOL FIRST
Not checking core (rectal) temperature
Assuming dry skin means dehydration not heat stroke
Not recognizing that medications can contribute
Inadequate cooling methods (ice packs alone are not enough)

allDiagnoses.name: Heat Stroke
allDiagnoses.rationale: Core temp 106.2°F + AMS + hot dry skin + outdoor construction work during heat wave = exertional heat stroke.

allDiagnoses.name: Heat Exhaustion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Crossed the line to heat stroke with temp >104°F and altered mental status.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypoglycemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Diabetic patient - must check glucose immediately, but clinical picture fits heat stroke.

allDiagnoses.name: Cardiac Arrhythmia/MI
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sudden collapse could be cardiac - need EKG, but heat stroke explains everything.

allDiagnoses.name: Seizure
allDiagnoses.rationale: No witnessed seizure activity. Heat stroke explains AMS.

allDiagnoses.name: Stroke (CVA)
allDiagnoses.rationale: AMS requires stroke consideration - CT if not improving with cooling.

allDiagnoses.name: Sepsis
allDiagnoses.rationale: No infectious source identified, environmental exposure clear.

male-55-worker.oldcartsNarrative: "EMS reports: 55-year-old male found unresponsive by coworkers at outdoor construction site around noon. It's day 3 of a heat wave with temps over 105°F. Patient was working on road paving crew. Coworkers say he complained of headache and dizziness about an hour before collapse, then suddenly became confused and fell over. Was working since 6 AM with minimal breaks. Skin is hot and dry. History of HTN and diabetes per coworkers."

historyQuestions.answer: He was spreading asphalt, said his head was killing him, looked confused, then just went down.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prodrome of headache and confusion classic for heat stroke

historyQuestions.text: How long had he been working?
historyQuestions.answer: Started at 6 AM. Collapsed around noon. About 6 hours straight.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prolonged exertion in heat dramatically increases heat stroke risk

historyQuestions.answer: He had a water bottle but didn't drink much. Said he wasn't thirsty.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Inadequate hydration + thirst suppression indicates severe heat stress

historyQuestions.answer: We're supposed to but we're behind schedule. He kept working.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No rest breaks = no opportunity for physiologic cooling

historyQuestions.answer: A water pill, metformin, and something for blood pressure.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diuretics impair heat adaptation; beta-blockers limit cardiovascular compensation

historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure and diabetes. His wife says he doesn't take care of himself.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diabetes and HTN are risk factors for heat illness

historyQuestions.answer: He had beers last night. We all did, it was so hot.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol causes dehydration and impairs thermoregulation

historyQuestions.answer: His wife says no allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for treatment - may need sedation for cooling

historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_current_job_title.relevance: Asphalt work is extremely high-risk for heat illness. Hot asphalt radiates additional heat on top of ambient temperature.

occupation_current_work_outdoors.answer: All outdoor work. In direct sun, standing on hot asphalt.
occupation_current_work_outdoors.relevance: Direct sun exposure plus heat radiating from asphalt can create effective temperatures 20-30°F higher than ambient.

air_quality_local_air_quality.answer: It's the third day over 105°F. Heat wave.
air_quality_local_air_quality.relevance: Heat waves are deadly - cumulative heat exposure over multiple days dramatically increases risk.

housing_ventilation.answer: His trailer doesn't have good A/C. He said he couldn't sleep because of the heat.
housing_ventilation.relevance: Inability to cool down at night prevents physiologic recovery and compounds heat stress.

chemicals_medications_supplements.answer: Takes a diuretic and blood pressure medication.
chemicals_medications_supplements.relevance: Diuretics impair heat adaptation by causing volume depletion. Beta-blockers limit cardiac compensation.

occupation_current_coworkers_sick.answer: A few other guys have been feeling bad too. Couple went home early.
occupation_current_coworkers_sick.relevance: Multiple workers affected indicates dangerous environmental conditions - workplace safety issue.

physicalExam.finding: Unresponsive to voice, withdraws to pain. GCS 8 (E2V2M4).
physicalExam.reasoning: AMS is required for heat stroke diagnosis

physicalExam.finding: Core temp 106.2°F (rectal). BP 88/50, HR 130, RR 32.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Core temp >104°F with AMS = Heat Stroke by definition

physicalExam.finding: Skin is HOT and DRY. No sweating despite high temperature.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hot DRY skin indicates failure of sweating mechanism - classic heat stroke

physicalExam.finding: GCS 8. Decorticate posturing to pain. No focal deficits but globally impaired.
physicalExam.reasoning: Global encephalopathy without focal signs suggests metabolic/toxic cause

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, hyperdynamic. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hyperdynamic circulation is compensatory response to heat stress

physicalExam.finding: Pupils 4mm, sluggishly reactive
physicalExam.reasoning: Sluggish pupils indicate CNS dysfunction from hyperthermia

labs.name: Point-of-care glucose
labs.reasoning: Must rule out hypoglycemia immediately - glucose is normal here

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Hypernatremia, hyperkalemia, AKI from dehydration and possible rhabdo

labs.name: CPK
labs.reasoning: Markedly elevated - rhabdomyolysis from heat injury

labs.name: Coagulation studies
labs.reasoning: Early DIC developing - common complication of heat stroke

imaging.name: CT Head
imaging.result: No intracranial hemorrhage or mass. Mild cerebral edema.
imaging.reasoning: Rules out stroke; cerebral edema is secondary to hyperthermia

procedures.name: IMMEDIATE cold water immersion
procedures.result: Patient placed in ice bath. Temperature dropping.
procedures.reasoning: COOL FIRST - this is the definitive treatment. Every minute matters.

procedures.name: Large bore IV access x2
procedures.result: Two 18g IVs placed. Fluid resuscitation initiated.
procedures.reasoning: IV access for fluids and medications

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.result: Placed. Initial output 50mL dark urine.
procedures.reasoning: Monitor UOP; dark urine suggests myoglobinuria

procedures.name: Continuous core temperature monitoring
procedures.result: Rectal probe placed for continuous monitoring.
procedures.reasoning: Must monitor cooling progress - goal temp <102°F

consults.name: ICU admission
consults.result: ICU bed arranged for continuous monitoring.
consults.reasoning: Requires ICU level care for multi-organ monitoring

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Admitted to ICU for heat stroke management.
disposition.reasoning: Heat stroke requires ICU monitoring

actualDiagnosis.name: Exertional Heat Stroke
keyFindings: Road construction worker on hot asphalt during heat wave
6 hours continuous work without adequate breaks
No A/C at home (couldn't recover overnight)
teachingPoints: Heat stroke = core temp >104°F + altered mental status
Hot DRY skin indicates sweating failure (though exertional can still sweat initially)
COOL FIRST, transport second - every minute of hyperthermia causes damage
Cold water immersion is gold standard (drops temp 0.2°C/min)
Risk factors: age >50, obesity, medications (diuretics, anticholinergics, beta-blockers)
Multi-day heat waves are especially deadly (no overnight recovery)
Mortality 10-50% depending on time to cooling
Report to OSHA - this is a workplace safety issue

closureScript.diagnosis: Your husband has heat stroke - a life-threatening emergency where his body temperature got dangerously high. His core temperature was over 106 degrees, which is why he's unconscious.
closureScript.plan: We're cooling him down aggressively right now with ice water. He's being admitted to the ICU where we'll monitor him closely. We're also treating the damage to his kidneys and muscles that happened from the heat.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Heat stroke can cause damage to the brain, kidneys, liver, and muscles. The next 24-48 hours are critical. We'll know more about any lasting effects as he recovers.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: We're also going to report this to workplace safety authorities. This should not have happened - working on hot asphalt during a heat wave without adequate breaks and hydration is dangerous. Other workers were affected too.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Acute onset of symptoms brought patient to emergency department.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Symptoms severe enough to warrant emergency care. Patient is anxious.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the temperature was 101°F and patient was alert?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same heat exposure but temp 101°F, patient is sweating, oriented but weak

chiefComplaint.original: Found collapsed at outdoor job site
chiefComplaint.modified: Construction worker feeling weak, dizzy, nauseous after working in heat. He's awake and talking.

feedback.correct: Heat exhaustion = temp <104°F, normal mental status, sweating intact. Treatment: remove from heat, passive cooling, oral or IV fluids. Can progress to heat stroke if not treated. MONITOR for AMS which signals progression.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of heat exhaustion. What's the key difference from heat stroke?
feedback.incorrect: Heat exhaustion vs heat stroke: EXHAUSTION = temp <104, NORMAL mental status, sweating present. STROKE = temp >104, ALTERED mental status. Treat exhaustion with fluids, rest, passive cooling. Monitor closely - can progress to stroke.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Heat exhaustion = precursor state, treatable with fluids and rest, normal mentation. Heat stroke = emergency, requires aggressive cooling, has AMS. Mental status is the key distinguishing feature.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was an elderly person found in a hot apartment?
whatIfScenarios.description: 85-year-old found in apartment without AC during heat wave

chiefComplaint.original: Found collapsed at outdoor job site
chiefComplaint.modified: 85-year-old found unresponsive in hot apartment. No AC. Lives alone. Neighbors hadn't seen her in 2 days.

feedback.correct: Classic heat stroke: elderly, homebound, no AC, often on medications that impair thermoregulation. DRY skin is more common (sweating fails). Same treatment - aggressive cooling. Higher mortality than exertional. Social factors are critical - welfare checks save lives.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of classic heat stroke. How does it differ from exertional?
feedback.incorrect: Classic (non-exertional) heat stroke: elderly, homebound, medications (anticholinergics, diuretics, beta-blockers). Multi-day heat waves especially deadly - no overnight recovery. Dry skin more common (sweating failure). Same cooling treatment. Prevention: welfare checks, cooling centers.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Exertional heat stroke = young, healthy, during exercise, may still sweat initially. Classic = elderly, homebound, medications, dry skin, multi-day exposure. Classic has higher mortality.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient developed dark urine and rising creatinine?
whatIfScenarios.description: Heat stroke patient with CK 85,000, dark urine, creatinine rising

chiefComplaint.original: Found collapsed at outdoor job site
chiefComplaint.modified: Heat stroke patient - now CK 85,000, dark tea-colored urine, creatinine 3.2 (was 1.0)

feedback.correct: Rhabdomyolysis common in heat stroke - muscle breakdown from heat injury. Myoglobin damages kidneys. Treatment: aggressive IV fluids to maintain UOP >200-300 mL/hr, monitor potassium (can be deadly), may need dialysis. Goal is to flush myoglobin before it damages kidneys.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of rhabdomyolysis. What's the kidney-protective strategy?
feedback.incorrect: Heat stroke → rhabdomyolysis (CK >10,000) → myoglobinuric AKI. Myoglobin precipitates in tubules causing acute tubular necrosis. Treatment: aggressive IV fluids (goal UOP 200-300 mL/hr), monitor K+ (hyperkalemia kills), may need dialysis. Bicarbonate alkalinization controversial but sometimes used.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Uncomplicated heat stroke = cool and hydrate. Heat stroke + rhabdo = much more aggressive fluids, monitor potassium, may need dialysis. CK level and urine output guide management.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.
Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.

child-3-lead.name: Jason Thomas
child-3-lead.chiefComplaint: He's not talking like other kids his age and he's been very irritable

missedConsequences.Lead Poisoning: Continued exposure causes permanent neurocognitive damage, behavioral disorders, and learning disabilities. Severe cases cause encephalopathy, seizures, coma.

commonPitfalls: Attributing symptoms to 'normal toddler behavior'
Not asking about housing age and condition
Missing the connection between pica and lead exposure
Not testing siblings who share the same exposure
Treating the child but not addressing the exposure source

allDiagnoses.name: Lead Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Old house + pica + developmental delay + irritability + anemia = lead poisoning until proven otherwise.

allDiagnoses.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder
allDiagnoses.rationale: Need to rule out lead first. Social engagement appears normal per mother.

allDiagnoses.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common in toddlers with pica. Often co-exists with lead poisoning.

allDiagnoses.name: Speech/Language Delay
allDiagnoses.rationale: Present, but lead may be the cause.

allDiagnoses.name: Hearing Loss
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must rule out as cause of speech delay.

allDiagnoses.name: Developmental Delay (other causes)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Need to identify and treat lead first.

child-3-lead.oldcartsNarrative: "I'm worried about my son. He's 3 years old but barely says any words - maybe 5 or 6. Other kids his age are talking in sentences. He's also been very irritable and cranky lately, throws tantrums constantly. He doesn't want to eat much but I keep finding him chewing on things - the windowsill, paint chips, even dirt from the yard. We moved into an older house about a year ago. The landlord said he'd fix things but never did."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about his speech development
historyQuestions.answer: He only says maybe 5-6 words. No sentences. He used to babble more as a baby.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Regression or stagnation in speech development is concerning for lead toxicity

historyQuestions.answer: He's so irritable and cranky. Constant tantrums. He wasn't like this before we moved.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Behavioral changes after moving to old house are a red flag

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned he chews on things?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! He chews on the windowsills, picks at paint chips, eats dirt. I try to stop him but he keeps doing it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: PICA (eating non-food items) is both a risk factor and symptom of lead poisoning

historyQuestions.answer: Poor. He doesn't want to eat regular food but chews on everything else.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Poor appetite and pica suggest nutritional deficiency and possible lead toxicity

historyQuestions.answer: Actually yes, he's been constipated. Cries when he tries to go.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Constipation and abdominal pain are GI symptoms of lead toxicity

historyQuestions.answer: Terrible. Up at night, restless, sometimes he seems confused when he wakes up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sleep disturbance and confusion can indicate lead encephalopathy

historyQuestions.text: Is he on any medications or vitamins?
historyQuestions.answer: He's supposed to take a multivitamin but he spits it out. Nothing else.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Iron/calcium can reduce lead absorption - important for treatment

historyQuestions.answer: Not that I know of. He's never really been sick before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before chelation therapy

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems? Was he premature?
historyQuestions.answer: No, he was born full-term, healthy. No problems until this past year.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms normal baseline - symptoms developed after exposure

historyQuestions.text: How was his development before the move?
historyQuestions.answer: He was normal! Babbling, saying 'mama' and 'dada', happy baby. It's like he's gone backwards since we moved.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Regression timeline correlating with move confirms lead as cause

historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

housing_home_type.answer: We rent an old house. It was built in like 1920 or something.
housing_home_type.relevance: Houses built before 1978 likely contain lead paint. Pre-1950 homes have the highest lead content. This 1920s home is extremely high risk.

housing_renovations.answer: The landlord was supposed to fix things but paint is peeling everywhere. We tried to scrape some off ourselves.
housing_renovations.relevance: Deteriorating lead paint creates lead dust. DIY scraping without proper precautions creates massive lead exposure - dust settles on surfaces children touch.

housing_home_condition.answer: It's in bad shape. Paint peeling off walls and windowsills. Old windows that stick.
housing_home_condition.relevance: Friction surfaces like windows create lead dust with every open/close. Peeling paint accessible to child who has pica.

water_food_water_source.answer: City water but the pipes look really old and rusty.
water_food_water_source.relevance: Old pipes may contain lead. Lead leaches into water especially if pipes corrode.

occupation_current_job_title.answer: Dad works at an auto body shop doing paint and bodywork.
occupation_current_job_title.relevance: Auto body work can involve lead exposure. Workers can bring lead dust home on clothes ('take-home lead').

social_household_household_members.relevance: Sister shares the same exposure and needs testing too. All children in household must be screened.

geographic_places_lived.answer: We moved here about a year ago from a newer apartment.
geographic_places_lived.relevance: Timeline matches - symptoms developed after moving to old house. Previous apartment was likely safe.

physicalExam.finding: Irritable toddler, difficult to examine. Appears pale.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pallor suggests anemia; irritability is behavioral sign of lead toxicity

physicalExam.finding: BP 95/60, HR 110, normal for age. Temp 98.8°F.
physicalExam.reasoning: Vital signs relatively normal - no acute encephalopathy

physicalExam.finding: Says only 5-6 single words. Does not combine words. Follows simple commands. Good eye contact. No focal deficits.
physicalExam.reasoning: Speech delay significant - 3yo should have 200+ words and sentences

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mildly distended. Diffusely tender. No hepatosplenomegaly.
physicalExam.reasoning: Abdominal pain and distension can indicate lead colic

physicalExam.finding: Pale conjunctivae. No rashes. Note: grayish discoloration at gumline.
physicalExam.reasoning: Lead line (Burton line) at gumline is classic physical finding. Pallor suggests anemia.

physicalExam.finding: TMs normal bilaterally. Responds to sounds appropriately.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out hearing loss as cause of speech delay

labs.name: Blood Lead Level (BLL)
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: BLL 48 is severely elevated. Levels >45 require chelation therapy.

labs.name: CBC with smear
labs.result: Hgb 9.8, MCV 68 (microcytic). Smear shows basophilic stippling.
labs.reasoning: Microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling is classic for lead poisoning

labs.name: Iron studies
labs.result: Ferritin 8 (low), TIBC elevated. Iron deficiency present.
labs.reasoning: Iron deficiency is common with lead poisoning and enhances lead absorption

labs.name: Free Erythrocyte Protoporphyrin
labs.reasoning: FEP is elevated in lead poisoning and iron deficiency

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray
imaging.result: No radiopaque foreign bodies. Dense metaphyseal bands ('lead lines') visible at knee.
imaging.reasoning: Lead lines in bones indicate chronic exposure. Can see ingested paint chips.

imaging.name: Knee X-ray
imaging.result: Dense metaphyseal bands at distal femur and proximal tibia - 'lead lines'
imaging.reasoning: Lead lines at growth plates indicate chronic exposure in children

consults.name: Medical Toxicology/Lead Program
consults.result: Recommends chelation therapy. Home inspection required before discharge.
consults.reasoning: Toxicology guidance for chelation and remediation coordination

consults.name: Social Work/Public Health
consults.result: Will arrange home lead inspection and landlord notification.
consults.reasoning: Cannot send child back to contaminated home. Legal requirements for notification.

consults.name: Developmental Pediatrics
consults.result: Will follow for neurodevelopmental monitoring and early intervention.
consults.reasoning: Long-term developmental follow-up needed - damage may be permanent

disposition.name: Admit for chelation
disposition.result: Admitted for IV chelation therapy.
disposition.reasoning: BLL >45 requires inpatient chelation. Cannot discharge to contaminated home.

actualDiagnosis.name: Childhood Lead Poisoning
keyFindings: Lives in pre-1978 home (built 1920s) with deteriorating paint
Pica behavior - eating paint chips and dirt
Developmental regression/delay after moving to old home
teachingPoints: ALWAYS ask about housing age and condition in developmental concerns
Pre-1978 homes may contain lead paint; pre-1950 have highest levels
Pica is both risk factor AND symptom of lead poisoning
No safe level of lead - even low levels cause cognitive impairment
BLL >45 mcg/dL requires chelation; >70 is medical emergency
Must test ALL children in household
Cannot discharge child to contaminated home
Father's occupation (auto body) may contribute via take-home exposure
Lead effects on IQ and behavior may be PERMANENT

closureScript.diagnosis: Your son has lead poisoning. His blood lead level is very high - 48, when it should be less than 3.5. This is almost certainly from the old paint in your house.
closureScript.plan: We need to admit him to give medication that will help remove the lead from his body. This is called chelation therapy. More importantly, he cannot go back to that house until it's been inspected and the lead paint is properly removed. We're also going to test his sister.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Lead poisoning can cause permanent damage to the brain, affecting learning, behavior, and development. With treatment now and removing him from the exposure, we hope to prevent further damage, but some effects may be lasting. He'll need long-term follow-up.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is scary and you're worried about your housing situation. Our social worker will help you. Your landlord is legally required to address lead hazards. There may be assistance programs available. The most important thing is getting your children away from the lead.

patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Symptoms severe enough to warrant emergency care. Patient is anxious.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Young adult with supportive family and friends. Active social life.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the child presented with seizures and altered mental status?

chiefComplaint.modified: 4-year-old brought in seizing, confused, vomiting. Lives in pre-1978 housing, has pica.

feedback.correct: LEAD ENCEPHALOPATHY IS A MEDICAL EMERGENCY. BLL >70 with neurological symptoms. Treatment: IV chelation with BAL (dimercaprol) first, then add EDTA (never EDTA alone - mobilizes lead to brain). Manage cerebral edema (mannitol, elevate head). ICU admission. Mortality 25-30% even with treatment.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of emergency. What's the specific chelation approach?
feedback.incorrect: Lead encephalopathy: seizures, AMS, vomiting, papilledema = BLL likely >70 mcg/dL. EMERGENCY. Chelation: give BAL (dimercaprol) IM first, then EDTA. Never EDTA alone initially - it mobilizes lead and can worsen encephalopathy. ICU, manage cerebral edema. High mortality and survivors often have permanent damage.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: BLL 45-69 = urgent chelation (can use oral succimer). BLL >70 or encephalopathy = emergency IV chelation (BAL + EDTA). Symptoms determine treatment intensity.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was an adult construction worker?

chiefComplaint.modified: 45-year-old painter with fatigue, abdominal pain, joint aches. Works demolition on old buildings. Forgets things.

feedback.correct: Adult occupational lead: different sources (construction, demolition, battery work, shooting ranges) and different thresholds. Adults: BLL >50 mcg/dL = remove from work per OSHA. Chelation considered at >70-100 symptomatic. Also check ZPP. Concern for take-home exposure - test family members. Workers' comp claim.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of occupational exposure. What's the OSHA threshold?
feedback.incorrect: Adult occupational lead: OSHA standards require removal from lead work at BLL >50 mcg/dL. Chelation at higher levels than children (>70-100 if symptomatic). Check ZPP (zinc protoporphyrin). Take-home exposure: worker brings lead home on clothes - test family. High-risk jobs: demolition, painting, battery work, shooting ranges.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Children = more vulnerable, lower treatment thresholds, developmental concerns. Adults = higher thresholds, occupational source, focus on work removal and OSHA.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was pregnant with elevated blood lead?

chiefComplaint.modified: 28-year-old pregnant woman (20 weeks) found to have BLL 25 mcg/dL on prenatal screening. Works at ceramics factory.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Lead exposure during pregnancy - fetal risk
mustNotMiss: Risk of preterm birth and developmental effects

feedback.correct: Lead in pregnancy: crosses placenta, causes preterm birth, low birth weight, developmental effects. BLL >5 mcg/dL is concerning. Source removal essential (can't return to ceramics work). Chelation CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (mobilizes lead, may increase fetal exposure). Supportive care, nutrition (calcium, iron), close monitoring.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of fetal risk. Why is chelation contraindicated?
feedback.incorrect: Pregnancy + lead: lead crosses placenta freely. Risks: miscarriage, preterm birth, low birth weight, neurodevelopmental effects in child. Chelation is CONTRAINDICATED - mobilizes lead and may increase fetal transfer. Management: remove from source, optimize nutrition (calcium competes with lead absorption), monitor closely. Occupational removal.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Non-pregnant = chelation at appropriate levels. Pregnant = chelation contraindicated, focus on source removal. Pregnancy changes treatment approach entirely.

male-42-pesticide.name: Michael Rodriguez
male-42-pesticide.chiefComplaint: Feel really sick after spraying the fields - sweating, can't see right, stomach cramps

missedConsequences.Organophosphate Poisoning: Without atropine and pralidoxime, progressive cholinergic crisis leads to respiratory failure and death within hours.
missedConsequences.Carbamate Poisoning: Similar to OP but pralidoxime not needed. Must identify agent.

commonPitfalls: Not recognizing cholinergic toxidrome (SLUDGE/DUMBELS)
Not asking about pesticide exposure in agricultural workers
Waiting for lab confirmation before treating
Inadequate atropine dosing - need much higher doses than typical
Not decontaminating patient (secondary exposure to staff)

allDiagnoses.name: Organophosphate Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Farmworker + recent pesticide spraying + SLUDGE symptoms + miosis + bradycardia = OP poisoning until proven otherwise.

allDiagnoses.name: Carbamate Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Clinically identical to OP. Treatment similar except pralidoxime not needed.

allDiagnoses.name: Cholinergic Crisis (other)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider other cholinergic agents but pesticide exposure is clear.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Doesn't explain miosis, bradycardia, excessive secretions.

allDiagnoses.name: Heat Exhaustion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Bradycardia and miosis not consistent with heat illness.

male-42-pesticide.oldcartsNarrative: "I was spraying pesticides in the fields this morning and started feeling sick a few hours ago. I'm sweating like crazy, my stomach is cramping, I've had diarrhea and vomited several times. My vision is blurry and I can't stop drooling. My chest feels tight and I'm having trouble breathing. I feel weak all over. Some of the spray got on my skin and clothes - the sprayer was leaking."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me exactly what you were spraying
historyQuestions.answer: Pesticide for the crops. I don't know the exact name but it's for killing bugs. Strong smell.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Need to identify agent. Organophosphates are most dangerous agricultural pesticides.

historyQuestions.answer: The sprayer was leaking. Got all over my arms and soaked through my shirt. I could smell it strong.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dermal exposure through soaked clothing causes significant absorption

historyQuestions.answer: Started maybe 2 hours after I started spraying. Got worse over the last hour.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Onset 2-4 hours after exposure typical for dermal OP exposure

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your stomach symptoms
historyQuestions.answer: Terrible cramps. Vomited 4-5 times. Diarrhea. Can't stop.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GI hypermotility from cholinergic excess - part of SLUDGE

historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I can't stop drooling. My eyes are tearing too. Everything is wet.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Excessive secretions (salivation, lacrimation) = SLUDGE

historyQuestions.answer: Chest tight. Wheezing. Hard to take a deep breath. Feels like drowning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bronchospasm and bronchorrhea - can progress to respiratory failure

historyQuestions.answer: Just regular clothes. No mask or gloves. Too hot for all that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No PPE = massive exposure through skin and inhalation

historyQuestions.answer: Blood pressure pills sometimes. I don't remember the name.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Beta-blockers would worsen bradycardia - important for treatment

historyQuestions.answer: No.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical before atropine and pralidoxime administration

historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Nothing else.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cardiac history important given hemodynamic effects

historyQuestions.text: Have you been exposed to pesticides before?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I spray every week. Sometimes I feel a little sick but never this bad.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic exposure can cause cumulative cholinesterase depression

historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_current_job_title.answer: Farmworker. I spray pesticides and work the fields.
occupation_current_job_title.relevance: Farmworkers have highest risk of pesticide poisoning. Pesticide applicators at extreme risk.

occupation_current_workplace_exposures.answer: Pesticides, fertilizers. The stuff I was spraying today kills bugs on crops.
occupation_current_workplace_exposures.relevance: Insecticides include highly toxic organophosphates and carbamates that inhibit acetylcholinesterase.

occupation_current_ppe_use.answer: No gloves, no mask. Just regular clothes. It was hot.
occupation_current_ppe_use.relevance: Lack of PPE allowed massive dermal and inhalational exposure. This is a major OSHA violation.

chemicals_pesticides_home.answer: We keep some pesticides at home too for the garden.
chemicals_pesticides_home.relevance: Home storage of pesticides poses risk to family members.

social_household_others_sick.answer: My coworker who was with me is feeling sick too.
social_household_others_sick.relevance: Multiple workers affected indicates significant exposure event. Coworker needs evaluation.

physicalExam.finding: Acutely ill, diaphoretic, drooling, clothes still wet with chemical odor
physicalExam.reasoning: DECONTAMINATE IMMEDIATELY - patient still has pesticide on skin/clothes

physicalExam.finding: BP 100/60, HR 52 (BRADYCARDIA), RR 24, SpO2 91% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Bradycardia and hypoxia from cholinergic excess

physicalExam.finding: Pupils 1mm bilaterally (PINPOINT MIOSIS), profuse lacrimation
physicalExam.reasoning: MIOSIS is hallmark of cholinergic toxidrome - muscarinic effect

physicalExam.finding: Diffuse wheezing, copious secretions in airways, rhonchi throughout
physicalExam.reasoning: Bronchorrhea and bronchospasm - can cause respiratory failure

physicalExam.finding: Confused. Muscle fasciculations visible in arms and legs. Weakness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Fasciculations are nicotinic effect. Can progress to paralysis.

physicalExam.finding: Diaphoretic. Chemical odor on skin and clothes. No dermal burns.
physicalExam.reasoning: Must decontaminate to stop ongoing absorption

labs.name: RBC Cholinesterase
labs.reasoning: Confirms OP poisoning but DO NOT wait for results to treat

labs.name: ABG
labs.result: pH 7.32, pCO2 48, pO2 62 - respiratory acidosis with hypoxia

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.reasoning: Bronchorrhea can look like pulmonary edema

procedures.name: IMMEDIATE DECONTAMINATION
procedures.result: Remove all clothing. Wash skin with soap and water x 15 min.
procedures.reasoning: FIRST PRIORITY - stop ongoing absorption and protect staff

procedures.name: Atropine IV
procedures.result: 2mg IV given, repeated every 5 min until secretions dry. Required 12mg total.
procedures.reasoning: Atropine reverses muscarinic effects. Use much higher doses than typical - titrate to secretions.

procedures.name: Pralidoxime (2-PAM)
procedures.result: 1-2g IV over 30 min, then infusion
procedures.reasoning: Reactivates cholinesterase if given early (<24-48h). Critical for OP poisoning.

procedures.name: Prepare for intubation
procedures.result: Intubation equipment at bedside
procedures.reasoning: May need airway protection and ventilation

consults.name: Poison Control/Toxicology
consults.result: Confirms treatment plan. Recommends contacting employer for product identification.
consults.reasoning: Expert guidance for ongoing management

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: ICU for monitoring and continued treatment
disposition.reasoning: Requires ICU monitoring - can have delayed respiratory failure

actualDiagnosis.name: Organophosphate Pesticide Poisoning
keyFindings: No PPE - dermal and inhalational exposure
SLUDGE symptoms: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis
teachingPoints: SLUDGE/DUMBELS mnemonic for cholinergic toxidrome
Miosis + bradycardia + secretions = cholinergic until proven otherwise
DECONTAMINATE FIRST - protect yourself and stop absorption
Atropine doses in OP poisoning are MUCH higher than typical (may need 20-100+ mg)
Titrate atropine to SECRETIONS, not heart rate
Pralidoxime must be given early (<24-48h) to be effective
Respiratory failure is the main cause of death
Intermediate syndrome can occur 24-96h after apparent recovery
Report to OSHA - this is a workplace safety failure

closureScript.diagnosis: You have pesticide poisoning - specifically from a type called organophosphate. The pesticide you were exposed to is blocking an important chemical in your body and causing all these symptoms.
closureScript.plan: We're giving you two medications - atropine to reverse the effects on your secretions and heart, and pralidoxime to reactivate the enzyme the pesticide blocked. You're going to the ICU for close monitoring.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: This is serious - without treatment this can be fatal. Even after you feel better, there can be delayed effects, so we need to watch you closely for several days. Your coworker needs to come in too.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: This happened because you weren't given proper protective equipment. That's not acceptable and is against the law. We're required to report this. You should not be spraying pesticides without proper protection - gloves, suit, mask. Your employer is responsible for providing this.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Acute onset of symptoms brought patient to emergency department.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Symptoms severe enough to warrant emergency care. Patient is anxious.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if weakness returned 3 days after apparent recovery?

chiefComplaint.modified: Patient recovered from OP poisoning, now day 3 - developing neck weakness, can't lift head, breathing getting harder

feedback.correct: INTERMEDIATE SYNDROME: occurs 24-96h after OP poisoning, even after apparent recovery. Proximal muscle weakness (neck flexors first), respiratory muscles, cranial nerve palsies. NOT responsive to atropine/pralidoxime. Supportive care - may need mechanical ventilation for weeks.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of intermediate syndrome. Does atropine help?
feedback.incorrect: Intermediate syndrome: delayed complication 24-96h post-OP. Proximal muscle weakness progressing to respiratory failure. Not cholinergic - doesn't respond to atropine. Mechanism: persistent NMJ dysfunction. Treatment: supportive, may need ventilator for 1-3 weeks.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Acute cholinergic crisis = SLUDGE, responds to atropine. Intermediate syndrome = weakness days later, doesn't respond to atropine. Different timing and treatment.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the exposure was to carbamate insecticide instead?

chiefComplaint.modified: Same presentation - but product was carbaryl (Sevin) carbamate insecticide, not organophosphate

feedback.correct: Carbamates cause same cholinergic toxidrome as OPs but REVERSIBLE within hours (enzyme binding is temporary). Atropine still indicated. Pralidoxime controversial - not clearly needed since binding reverses spontaneously. Usually less severe, shorter course. Same decontamination needed.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of carbamate. How does treatment differ?
feedback.incorrect: Carbamates: same cholinergic symptoms as OPs but shorter duration. Enzyme inhibition spontaneously reverses. Atropine still needed. Pralidoxime controversial - some avoid it (may worsen carbaryl toxicity), others use it. Generally better prognosis than OPs. Same decontamination approach.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: OP = irreversible enzyme inhibition, pralidoxime needed, prolonged course. Carbamate = reversible, shorter duration, pralidoxime controversial. Agent identification matters.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was a nerve agent exposure (terrorism scenario)?

chiefComplaint.modified: Multiple patients from subway station with same symptoms - miosis, salivation, seizing. Terrorist attack suspected.

feedback.correct: Nerve agents (sarin, VX) = ultra-potent OPs. Same SLUDGE toxidrome. DECONTAMINATE before patients enter ED - staff protection critical. Same antidotes: atropine (high doses) + pralidoxime. Mark I kits (autoinjectors). Mass casualty triage. Notify authorities, activate disaster protocols.
feedback.partial: Good recognition. What about staff safety?
feedback.incorrect: Nerve agents: military-grade OPs. Same cholinergic syndrome. CRITICAL: decontaminate OUTSIDE before entry. Staff need full PPE. Treatment: atropine + pralidoxime (same as agricultural OPs). CHEMPACK has autoinjectors. Activate disaster protocols, notify authorities, expect secondary contamination.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Agricultural OP = single patient, standard precautions. Nerve agent = mass casualties, decontamination zone, disaster protocols, staff protection paramount. Setting changes everything.

family-co.name: Martinez Family
family-co.chiefComplaint: The whole family has headaches - we thought it was the flu but it won't go away
family-co.variantDescription: Carbon monoxide poisoning from faulty furnace

missedConsequences.Carbon Monoxide Poisoning: Continued exposure leads to myocardial ischemia, arrhythmias, seizures, coma, and death. Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome can occur weeks later.

commonPitfalls: Trusting pulse oximetry (reads falsely normal with CO)
Diagnosing 'viral syndrome' when multiple family members are sick
Not asking about home heating sources
Not recognizing that symptoms improve away from home
Sending patient back to contaminated home

allDiagnoses.name: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Multiple family members + headaches + winter + gas heating + symptoms better outside = CO poisoning until proven otherwise.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Syndrome/Influenza
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not explain rapid improvement when leaving house.

allDiagnoses.name: Tension Headache
allDiagnoses.rationale: Doesn't explain multiple family members simultaneously.

allDiagnoses.name: Natural Gas Leak
allDiagnoses.rationale: Gas leak could explain symptoms and poses explosion risk.

allDiagnoses.name: Food Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Timeline doesn't fit - symptoms for days, worse in morning.

family-co.oldcartsNarrative: "We all have terrible headaches that won't go away - me, my husband, and our two kids. We thought it was the flu but we don't have fevers. The headaches started about a week ago. They're worse in the morning and seem to get a little better when we go outside or go to work/school. We've been feeling dizzy too, and my husband has been nauseous. The weird thing is our dog has been acting sick too - lethargic and won't eat. It's been cold so we've been running the heater constantly."

historyQuestions.answer: All four of us - me, my husband, and both kids (ages 8 and 12). Even our dog is sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: MULTIPLE family members + pet sick = environmental cause, not infectious

historyQuestions.text: Is there a pattern to when symptoms are better or worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Worse in the morning. Better when we leave the house for work or school. Come back when we get home.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL CLUE - symptoms improve AWAY from home, worse with time IN home

historyQuestions.answer: Dull, throbbing, all over. Like a band around my head. Constant.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diffuse headache is most common symptom of CO poisoning

historyQuestions.answer: Dizzy, confused sometimes, my husband has been nauseous. I feel weak.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Dizziness, confusion, nausea = progressive CO toxicity

historyQuestions.text: How are you heating your home?
historyQuestions.answer: Gas furnace. It's been running constantly because it's so cold. It's pretty old.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Old gas furnace running constantly = most common source of home CO poisoning

historyQuestions.text: Do you have carbon monoxide detectors?
historyQuestions.answer: We have one but I think the battery died. We kept meaning to replace it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-functioning CO detector allowed poisoning to go undetected

historyQuestions.text: Any recent work on the furnace or house?
historyQuestions.answer: No, but the furnace has been making a weird noise lately.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Malfunctioning furnace producing CO

historyQuestions.answer: I take something for blood pressure. My husband takes nothing. Kids are healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review; cardiac meds important given CO cardiotoxicity

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies in the family?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies for any of us.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for treatment planning

historyQuestions.text: Any medical problems in the family?
historyQuestions.answer: I have high blood pressure. My husband has asthma. Kids are healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Asthma and cardiac disease increase CO poisoning risk

historyQuestions.text: Is anyone in the house pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: No, definitely not.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pregnancy is indication for hyperbaric oxygen - fetal Hgb binds CO more tightly

historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

housing_heating_type.answer: Gas furnace. It's old, maybe 20 years. Been running constantly this week because it's so cold.
housing_heating_type.relevance: Old gas furnace running constantly is #1 cause of residential CO poisoning. Cracked heat exchanger or blocked flue causes CO buildup.

housing_ventilation.answer: We keep windows closed because of the cold. House is sealed up tight.
housing_ventilation.relevance: Poor ventilation allows CO to accumulate to dangerous levels.

housing_home_condition.answer: Old house, about 50 years. Things need fixing but we can't afford it.
housing_home_condition.relevance: Older homes have older appliances and may lack modern safety features.

social_household_household_members.answer: Four of us - two adults, two kids (8 and 12). Plus our dog.
social_household_household_members.relevance: ALL household members affected - environmental cause confirmed. Children and pets more susceptible.

social_household_pets_home.answer: Yes, a dog. He's been acting sick too - lethargic, not eating.
social_household_pets_home.relevance: Sick pet is classic clue for CO poisoning - smaller animals affected first due to faster metabolism.

chemicals_drug_exposure.answer: No other exposures. We don't have a car in the garage or anything like that.
chemicals_drug_exposure.relevance: Rules out car exhaust. Furnace is the source.

physicalExam.finding: Alert but appears fatigued. Complains of persistent headache.
physicalExam.reasoning: May look deceptively well despite significant CO exposure

physicalExam.finding: BP 138/88, HR 102, RR 20, Temp 98.6°F, SpO2 98% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: WARNING: Pulse ox reads FALSELY NORMAL with CO - it cannot distinguish COHb from O2Hb

physicalExam.finding: Skin color appears normal. No classic 'cherry red' discoloration.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cherry red color is actually rare and usually only seen post-mortem

physicalExam.finding: Alert and oriented but slow to respond. Mild confusion. No focal deficits.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cognitive impairment from CO effect on brain

physicalExam.finding: Tachycardic, regular. No murmurs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia is compensatory response to tissue hypoxia

labs.name: Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) level
labs.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC: COHb 22% confirms significant CO poisoning. Must use co-oximetry, not pulse ox.

labs.name: ABG with co-oximetry
labs.reasoning: PaO2 can be normal despite severe CO poisoning. MUST check COHb.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: CO causes myocardial ischemia. Elevated troponin indicates cardiac injury.

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Sinus tachycardia. ST depressions in V4-V6.
imaging.reasoning: EKG changes indicate myocardial ischemia from CO

procedures.name: 100% oxygen via non-rebreather
procedures.result: Started immediately
procedures.reasoning: High-flow O2 accelerates CO elimination. Half-life of COHb drops from 4-6h to 60-90 min on 100% O2.

procedures.name: Evaluate for hyperbaric oxygen
procedures.result: Criteria met - arranging HBO therapy
procedures.reasoning: HBO indicated for: COHb >25%, loss of consciousness, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy, severe symptoms

consults.name: Toxicology/Hyperbaric Medicine
consults.result: Recommends HBO for patient and family members with elevated COHb
consults.reasoning: Expert guidance on HBO therapy

consults.name: Fire Department notification
consults.result: Fire department dispatched to home with CO meters
consults.reasoning: MUST evacuate home and identify source. Cannot return until cleared.

disposition.name: Admit for HBO therapy
disposition.result: Admitted. Family members being evaluated.
disposition.reasoning: HBO therapy and monitoring required. All family members need COHb levels.

actualDiagnosis.name: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
keyFindings: Multiple family members with same symptoms
Symptoms worse at home, better when away
teachingPoints: When multiple family members are sick, think ENVIRONMENTAL
Sick pets are a classic clue - 'canary in the coal mine'
Pulse oximetry is FALSELY NORMAL in CO poisoning
Must order COHb level (co-oximetry) specifically
Symptoms improve away from exposure source
Winter months + gas heating = high risk period
Half-life of COHb: 4-6h on room air, 60-90 min on 100% O2, 20 min with HBO
Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome can occur 2-40 days after exposure
NEVER send patient back to contaminated home

closureScript.diagnosis: Your family has carbon monoxide poisoning. Carbon monoxide is an invisible, odorless gas that's been building up in your home, probably from your old furnace. This is why everyone - including your dog - has been feeling sick.
closureScript.plan: We're giving you high-concentration oxygen to clear the carbon monoxide from your blood. You may need treatment in a special hyperbaric oxygen chamber. We need to test your husband and children too. The fire department is going to your home right now to find the source and make sure it's safe.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: You cannot go back to your home until it's been cleared by the fire department and the furnace has been inspected or replaced. Carbon monoxide can cause brain and heart damage - some effects might show up weeks later, so we'll need to follow up.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is scary and disruptive. But if you had stayed in that house much longer, this could have been fatal. All of you will recover, but we need to make sure the source is fixed before anyone goes back. Your landlord is responsible for fixing this.

guidelineReferences.name: UHMS Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy Indications: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
keyPoints: HBO criteria: LOC, COHb >25%, cardiac ischemia, neurologic symptoms, pregnancy
100% O2 via NRB mask for all patients
Normobaric O2 half-life of COHb: 4-5 hours
HBO half-life of COHb: 20-30 minutes
Consider HBO for delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome prevention

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Carbon Monoxide Poisoning Prevention
guidelineReferences.citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
keyPoints: CO detectors on every level of home
Annual heating system inspection
Never run car in enclosed garage
Symptoms: headache, dizziness, confusion, nausea
Leading cause of poisoning deaths in US

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was comatose?
whatIfScenarios.description: Found unresponsive, COHb 45%, running car in closed garage

chiefComplaint.original: I've been having headaches every morning for a week
chiefComplaint.modified: Found unconscious in garage with car running. COHb 45%.

feedback.correct: Severe CO poisoning (coma, COHb >25%, cardiac involvement) = emergent HBO therapy. Intubate if needed. Also evaluate for intentional exposure (suicide attempt) - will need psychiatric evaluation. High risk for delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of severity. What's the definitive treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Severe CO poisoning (coma, COHb >25%, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy): EMERGENT HBO therapy. Criteria for HBO: LOC, COHb >25%, cardiac involvement, neurologic symptoms, pregnancy. Closed garage + running car: evaluate for intentional exposure → psychiatric consult.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Mild CO (headache, COHb <15-20%) = normobaric 100% O2, may resolve. Severe CO (coma, high COHb, cardiac involvement) = emergent HBO therapy. Severity determines treatment intensity.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was a suicide attempt?
whatIfScenarios.description: Young patient found in car with note, COHb 38%

chiefComplaint.original: I've been having headaches every morning for a week
chiefComplaint.modified: 22-year-old found in running car with suicide note. COHb 38%.

feedback.correct: Treat the CO poisoning (HBO if indicated) AND the psychiatric emergency. Patient needs 1:1 observation, psychiatric consultation, and safety evaluation. Can't discharge until medically AND psychiatrically cleared. Multidisciplinary care required.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of dual emergency. What psychiatric precautions are needed?
feedback.incorrect: Intentional CO poisoning = medical AND psychiatric emergency. Treat CO toxicity (HBO if indicated). Simultaneously: 1:1 observation (suicide precautions), psychiatric consult, assess for other ingestions. Cannot discharge until BOTH medically stable AND psychiatrically cleared.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Accidental CO = treat poisoning, fix source, safe discharge. Intentional CO = treat poisoning AND psychiatric emergency. Both need medical treatment, but intentional exposure requires comprehensive psychiatric care.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if patient developed confusion 3 weeks after CO exposure?
whatIfScenarios.description: Previously treated CO poisoning, now returned with cognitive decline, parkinsonism

chiefComplaint.original: I've been having headaches every morning for a week
chiefComplaint.modified: I was treated for CO poisoning 3 weeks ago. Now I'm confused and can't walk right.

feedback.correct: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome (DNS) occurs 2-40 days after CO exposure in up to 40% of patients. Features: cognitive decline, personality change, parkinsonism, incontinence. MRI shows white matter changes. Treatment: may benefit from repeat HBO, supportive care. Can take months to improve.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of DNS. What's the timeline and treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome (DNS): occurs 2-40 days after apparent recovery from CO poisoning. Up to 40% of severe CO poisoning. Symptoms: cognitive impairment, personality changes, parkinsonism, incontinence. MRI: white matter lesions. HBO may help even delayed. Prognosis variable.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Acute CO = treat immediately, often full recovery. DNS = delayed deterioration after lucid interval, white matter damage, may persist. All CO poisoning patients should be warned about DNS and followed closely.

guidelineReferences.name: CDC Ciguatera Guidelines
keyPoints: GI + neurological symptoms
Paradoxical temperature reversal pathognomonic
No specific antidote - supportive care

female-42-fish.name: David Lee
female-42-fish.chiefComplaint: Started vomiting after dinner on vacation and now I have bizarre tingling sensations

missedConsequences.Ciguatera Poisoning: Symptoms can persist for weeks to months. Cardiovascular complications can occur. Misdiagnosis leads to unnecessary testing.

commonPitfalls: Diagnosing as 'food poisoning' without asking what fish was eaten
Not recognizing temperature reversal as pathognomonic
Ordering expensive neurological workups for peripheral neuropathy
Not warning about avoiding fish, alcohol, and nuts (can trigger recurrence)

allDiagnoses.name: Ciguatera Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Caribbean vacation + reef fish (barracuda) + GI symptoms + temperature reversal + bradycardia = classic ciguatera.

allDiagnoses.name: Scombroid Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: Scombroid causes flushing and histamine symptoms, not temperature reversal.

allDiagnoses.name: Paralytic Shellfish Poisoning
allDiagnoses.rationale: She ate fish not shellfish.

allDiagnoses.name: Botulism
allDiagnoses.rationale: No descending paralysis, pupils normal.

allDiagnoses.name: Guillain-Barré Syndrome
allDiagnoses.rationale: Acute onset with GI symptoms and temperature reversal not consistent.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not explain neurological symptoms or temperature reversal.

female-42-fish.oldcartsNarrative: "I got sick a few hours after dinner last night. We're on vacation in the Caribbean. I had terrible nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal cramps. But then the really weird stuff started - my hands and feet are tingling, and here's the strangest thing: when I touch cold water, it feels like it's burning hot! I've never experienced anything like it. My husband ate the same fish and he's sick too."

historyQuestions.text: What did you eat for dinner?
historyQuestions.answer: We had fresh-caught barracuda at a local restaurant. It was delicious at the time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Barracuda is classic ciguatera-associated reef fish. Other: grouper, snapper, amberjack, moray eel.

historyQuestions.answer: Vomiting 5-6 times, watery diarrhea, terrible abdominal cramps. Started about 6 hours after eating.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GI symptoms typically start 6-12 hours after ingestion

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the cold water feeling hot
historyQuestions.answer: When I run cold water over my hands, it feels like it's burning! Hot water feels cold. It's so bizarre.
historyQuestions.reasoning: TEMPERATURE REVERSAL (cold allodynia) is PATHOGNOMONIC for ciguatera. Almost nothing else causes this.

historyQuestions.answer: Hands and feet are tingling and numb. Also around my mouth.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Perioral and extremity paresthesias are classic neurological symptoms

historyQuestions.answer: My teeth feel loose when I bite down. And my muscles ache.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Loose teeth sensation and myalgias are recognized ciguatera symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Is your husband having the same symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, he ate the same fish and has GI symptoms and tingling too. Not as bad as me though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Both people who ate the fish are sick = the fish was the source

historyQuestions.answer: Blood pressure medicine - lisinopril. And a statin for cholesterol.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for treatment - cardiac effects may be exacerbated

historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before treatment with mannitol or other medications

historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure and high cholesterol. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cardiac history important given bradycardia risk from ciguatoxin

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any alcohol today?
historyQuestions.answer: A couple beers at lunch, before this all started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Alcohol can worsen symptoms and must be avoided during recovery

historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

geographic_recent_travel.answer: We're on vacation in the Caribbean - St. Thomas.
geographic_recent_travel.relevance: Caribbean is highest-risk area for ciguatera. Also Hawaii, Pacific islands, Florida Keys. Ciguatera is most common cause of fish poisoning worldwide.

water_food_raw_foods.answer: We had fresh-caught barracuda at a beachside restaurant. The locals recommended it.
water_food_raw_foods.relevance: Barracuda is classic ciguatera fish - large reef-dwelling predator that accumulates toxin. The larger the fish, the higher the toxin load.

geographic_endemic_areas.answer: We've been eating local fish all week but this is the first barracuda.
geographic_endemic_areas.relevance: Reef fish in tropical waters can carry ciguatoxin. Barracuda, grouper, snapper, amberjack are highest risk.

hobbies_hunting_fishing_rec.answer: Some local fishermen caught it that morning. They said it was the freshest you could get.
hobbies_hunting_fishing_rec.relevance: Freshness doesn't matter - ciguatoxin is heat-stable and not destroyed by cooking or freezing. Cannot taste it.

physicalExam.finding: BP 108/68, HR 58 (BRADYCARDIA), Temp 98.2°F. Orthostatic changes present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bradycardia and hypotension are cardiovascular effects of ciguatoxin

physicalExam.finding: Decreased sensation to light touch in glove-stocking distribution. Cold stimulus perceived as burning.
physicalExam.reasoning: Temperature reversal on exam confirms pathognomonic symptom

physicalExam.finding: Strength intact throughout. No weakness.
physicalExam.reasoning: Motor function typically preserved (unlike paralytic shellfish poisoning)

physicalExam.finding: Diffusely tender, hyperactive bowel sounds. No peritoneal signs.
physicalExam.reasoning: GI involvement but no surgical abdomen

physicalExam.finding: Mild dehydration - dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor.
physicalExam.reasoning: Dehydration from GI losses

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 3.4 (mildly low from vomiting/diarrhea), Cr 1.1 (mild AKI from dehydration)
labs.reasoning: Check electrolytes - hypokalemia from GI losses. Renal function for dehydration. Replete potassium if low.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Usually normal in ciguatera. Not an infectious process.

labs.name: LFTs
labs.reasoning: Hepatic involvement rare but can occur. Helps rule out other toxins.

labs.name: Troponin (if cardiac symptoms)
labs.reasoning: Consider if chest pain or significant arrhythmia. Myocarditis has been reported rarely.

labs.name: Ciguatoxin testing (research only)
labs.result: Not clinically available - diagnosis is CLINICAL.
labs.reasoning: No commercial test available. Diagnosis is clinical based on history (reef fish + endemic area + temperature reversal).

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Sinus bradycardia at 55 bpm. Normal PR/QRS/QT. No ST changes.
imaging.reasoning: CRITICAL: Monitor for bradycardia, heart block, and arrhythmias. Ciguatoxin affects sodium channels and can cause cardiac conduction abnormalities.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray (if respiratory symptoms)
imaging.reasoning: Only if respiratory distress, which is rare in ciguatera.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.result: Peripheral IV placed for fluid resuscitation.
procedures.reasoning: IV access for fluids and potential treatment.

procedures.name: IV fluid resuscitation
procedures.result: 2L NS given with improvement in orthostatic hypotension and heart rate. K replaced.
procedures.reasoning: Correct dehydration from GI losses. Replete electrolytes as needed.

procedures.name: Continuous cardiac monitoring
procedures.result: Placed on telemetry for bradycardia monitoring.
procedures.reasoning: Monitor for arrhythmias - bradycardia, heart block, ventricular ectopy can occur.

treatments.name: IV Mannitol 1g/kg (controversial)
treatments.result: Mannitol 1g/kg IV given over 30-45 minutes for neurological symptoms. Some improvement noted.
treatments.reasoning: Mannitol may help neurological symptoms if given early (<24h). Mechanism unclear. Evidence is mixed (some studies positive, some negative). Consider in severe neurological cases.

treatments.name: Antiemetics
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV for nausea with good effect.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic relief for nausea and vomiting.

treatments.name: Avoid opioids if possible
treatments.result: Using non-opioid analgesia. Opioids may worsen bradycardia.
treatments.reasoning: Opioids can exacerbate bradycardia from ciguatoxin. Use NSAIDs or acetaminophen for pain if possible.

treatments.name: Atropine (for symptomatic bradycardia)
treatments.result: Not needed - patient asymptomatic from bradycardia at current HR. Available at bedside.
treatments.reasoning: Keep atropine available for symptomatic bradycardia. Standard ACLS dosing if needed.

treatments.name: Gabapentin (for chronic neuropathy)
treatments.result: Consider gabapentin 100-300mg TID for persistent paresthesias.
treatments.reasoning: For prolonged neurological symptoms (can last weeks-months). Helps with neuropathic pain and paresthesias.

treatments.name: Amitriptyline (for chronic symptoms)
treatments.result: Alternative: low-dose amitriptyline for persistent symptoms.
treatments.reasoning: TCAs have been used for chronic ciguatera symptoms. Use cautiously given cardiac effects.

consults.name: Poison Control Center
consults.result: Consulted regional poison control (1-800-222-1222) for management guidance.
consults.reasoning: Poison control can provide expert guidance on management, especially for severe cases.

consults.name: Cardiology (if significant arrhythmia)
consults.result: Not needed for this patient - mild bradycardia only.
consults.reasoning: Consult cardiology for significant bradycardia, heart block, or arrhythmias requiring intervention.

disposition.name: ED observation 6-8 hours
disposition.result: Observed in ED for 6 hours. HR improved to 65, orthostatics resolved, tolerating PO. Safe for discharge.
disposition.reasoning: Observe for cardiovascular complications. Most patients can be discharged once hemodynamically stable and tolerating PO.

disposition.name: Admission criteria
disposition.result: Would admit for: severe bradycardia (<50), heart block, hypotension unresponsive to fluids, respiratory distress, inability to tolerate PO.
disposition.reasoning: Most cases can be managed as outpatient. Admit for cardiovascular instability or severe symptoms.

disposition.name: Dietary avoidance counseling (CRITICAL)
disposition.result: CRITICAL counseling: Must avoid for 3-6 months: ALL fish, alcohol, nuts, caffeine. These can trigger symptom recurrence even after recovery.
disposition.reasoning: Recurrence of symptoms triggered by fish, alcohol, nuts is well-documented. Avoidance for months is essential. This is often the most difficult part for patients.

disposition.name: Exercise avoidance during acute phase
disposition.result: Avoid strenuous exercise for 1-2 weeks. Can also trigger symptom recurrence.
disposition.reasoning: Exercise and heat exposure can also trigger symptom recurrence during acute/subacute phase.

disposition.name: Return precautions
disposition.result: Return for: chest pain, palpitations, severe weakness, shortness of breath, worsening neurological symptoms.
disposition.reasoning: Cardiac complications can occur. Neurological symptoms typically improve gradually but can worsen in some cases.

disposition.name: Expected duration counseling
disposition.result: Counseled: GI symptoms resolve in 1-2 days. Neurological symptoms (tingling, temperature reversal) can last WEEKS to MONTHS. Most recover completely but it takes time.
disposition.reasoning: Set expectations - neurological recovery is slow. Some patients have persistent symptoms for >6 months. Reassure that complete recovery is expected.

disposition.name: Public health reporting
disposition.result: Reported to local health department. The restaurant and fishing area should be investigated.
disposition.reasoning: Report to public health - toxic reef areas may need to be identified to prevent additional cases. Restaurant should be notified.

disposition.name: Husband evaluation
disposition.result: Husband also evaluated and treated. Same precautions given.
disposition.reasoning: Anyone who ate the same fish needs evaluation, even if less symptomatic.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: Follow-up with PCP in 1-2 weeks. Consider neurology referral if symptoms persist >4 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Most recover without long-term follow-up. Neurology referral for persistent or severe neurological symptoms.

actualDiagnosis.name: Ciguatera Fish Poisoning
teachingPoints: Temperature reversal (cold feels hot) is PATHOGNOMONIC - almost nothing else causes this
High-risk fish: barracuda, grouper, snapper, amberjack, moray eel
Endemic areas: Caribbean, Hawaii, Pacific islands, Florida Keys
Toxin is heat-stable - cooking doesn't help
Cannot taste, smell, or see the toxin in fish
GI symptoms first (6-12 hrs), then neurological (12-24 hrs)
Symptoms can last WEEKS to MONTHS
AVOID fish, alcohol, and nuts after - can trigger symptom recurrence
No specific antidote - supportive care
Report to public health - may indicate a toxic reef area

closureScript.diagnosis: You have ciguatera poisoning from the barracuda you ate. This is caused by a toxin that accumulates in large reef fish in tropical waters. The bizarre temperature reversal sensation is actually the classic sign of this type of poisoning.
closureScript.plan: We're giving you IV fluids for dehydration and will monitor you for a few hours. The GI symptoms should improve over the next day or two, but the neurological symptoms - the tingling and temperature reversal - can unfortunately last weeks to months. There's no specific antidote.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: You'll need to avoid eating fish, drinking alcohol, and eating nuts for the next few months - these can trigger your symptoms to come back. If you develop chest pain, severe weakness, or your heart feels like it's racing or skipping, come back immediately.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: Most people recover completely, but it can take time. The tingling will gradually improve. For the rest of your vacation and after you get home, stick to smaller fish from open ocean (like mahi-mahi or tuna) rather than large reef fish. Your husband should follow the same precautions.

male-32-dcs.name: Michael King
male-32-dcs.chiefComplaint: My shoulder is killing me and I feel weird after diving today

missedConsequences.Decompression Sickness: Untreated DCS can progress to permanent neurological damage, paralysis, and death. Symptoms can worsen rapidly.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about recent diving in any patient with joint pain
Dismissing symptoms as 'musculoskeletal strain'
Not recognizing that symptoms can be delayed up to 24 hours
Failing to arrange hyperbaric treatment emergently
Giving supplemental oxygen without calling DAN/hyperbaric

allDiagnoses.name: Decompression Sickness
allDiagnoses.rationale: Scuba diving + rapid ascent + joint pain + symptoms within 24h = DCS ('the bends') until proven otherwise.

allDiagnoses.name: Arterial Gas Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: If symptoms started immediately on surfacing, consider AGE - more emergent.

allDiagnoses.name: Musculoskeletal Strain
allDiagnoses.rationale: Timing with dive and character of pain point to DCS.

allDiagnoses.name: Shoulder Injury
allDiagnoses.rationale: No trauma. Diving history is key.

male-32-dcs.oldcartsNarrative: "My shoulder has been hurting since I got out of the water, and it's getting worse. It's a deep, boring pain. I also feel kind of dizzy and foggy. I was scuba diving this morning - did three dives. On the last one I came up a little fast because I was low on air. That was about 4 hours ago. I took ibuprofen but it's not helping at all. My buddy who was with me is fine."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your dives today
historyQuestions.answer: Three dives, deepest was 110 feet. Total bottom time probably 90 minutes. On the last dive I ran low on air and came up faster than I should have.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Deep dives + rapid ascent = high DCS risk. 110 feet is significant depth.

historyQuestions.text: How fast did you come up?
historyQuestions.answer: Faster than I should have. Skipped my safety stop because I was almost out of air.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Skipped safety stop = nitrogen didn't have time to off-gas properly

historyQuestions.answer: Shoulder started hurting maybe an hour after the last dive. The dizziness came on later.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Symptom onset within hours of diving is classic for DCS

historyQuestions.answer: Deep, boring, aching. Hard to pinpoint exactly. Not like a muscle strain. Ibuprofen didn't touch it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Deep boring joint pain unrelieved by NSAIDs is classic DCS description

historyQuestions.answer: Feel foggy, a little off balance. Hard to describe. Just not right.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Neurological symptoms suggest more serious Type II DCS - needs emergent treatment

historyQuestions.answer: Was supposed to fly home tomorrow. Our vacation ends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Flying after DCS dramatically worsens it. Must not fly.

historyQuestions.answer: No, I've been diving for years without problems. But I've never come up that fast before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Experienced diver who broke protocol

historyQuestions.answer: Blood pressure medicine - amlodipine. That's it.

keyFindings: Symptoms began within hours of diving
teachingPoints: ALWAYS ask about recent diving in unexplained joint pain or neurological symptoms
Type I DCS: joint pain, skin only. Type II: neurological, pulmonary, or cardiovascular
Any neurological symptoms = Type II = emergent hyperbaric treatment
Start 100% oxygen immediately - helps eliminate nitrogen
Call DAN (Divers Alert Network) 1-919-684-9111 for any diving emergency
DO NOT FLY patient - altitude worsens DCS
DO NOT delay treatment to get imaging (except CXR to rule out pneumothorax)
Hyperbaric oxygen is definitive treatment
Symptoms can be delayed up to 24 hours after diving

closureScript.diagnosis: You have decompression sickness - what divers call 'the bends.' When you came up too fast and skipped your safety stop, nitrogen that had dissolved in your blood under pressure formed bubbles. Those bubbles are causing your shoulder pain and your neurological symptoms.
closureScript.plan: You need hyperbaric oxygen treatment - going into a pressurized chamber where you'll breathe pure oxygen. This shrinks the bubbles and helps your body eliminate the nitrogen. We're transferring you now. You absolutely cannot fly home tomorrow.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: This can get worse quickly. The hyperbaric treatment is very effective if we do it promptly. After treatment, you'll need to wait at least 24-72 hours before flying, and you should not dive again until cleared by a dive medicine specialist.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this disrupts your travel plans. But flying right now could be life-threatening - the lower pressure at altitude would make the bubbles bigger. You'll need to contact your airline about changing your flight. DAN can help with logistics and may cover some costs if you have dive insurance.

keyPoints: Call DAN 24/7 hotline: 1-919-684-9111

IV fluids for hydration

missedConsequences.Esophageal Button Battery: Button batteries in esophagus cause liquefactive necrosis within 2 HOURS. Can perforate into aorta causing fatal hemorrhage. This is a TRUE EMERGENCY.

commonPitfalls: Waiting for symptoms before imaging when battery ingestion is suspected
Delaying removal - esophageal batteries must be removed within 2 hours

Confusing button battery with coin on X-ray
Giving anything by mouth (increases injury risk)

actualDiagnosis.name: Esophageal Button Battery Ingestion
keyFindings: Missing button battery from remote control
'Double halo' sign on X-ray (battery, not coin)
Battery lodged at aortic arch level (most common site)
teachingPoints: BUTTON BATTERIES IN ESOPHAGUS = 2 HOUR EMERGENCY - liquefactive necrosis starts immediately
20mm lithium batteries (CR2032) are most dangerous - perfect size to lodge in child esophagus
Current generation creates hydroxide ions → alkali burns → liquefactive necrosis
'Double halo' or 'step-off' sign on X-ray distinguishes battery from coin
National Battery Ingestion Hotline: 800-498-8666 (available 24/7)
DO NOT give honey, DO NOT induce vomiting, DO NOT wait for symptoms
Batteries in STOMACH can often be observed (pass in 48-72h) but esophageal = emergent
Aortoesophageal fistula from battery erosion can cause fatal hemorrhage
Prevention: secure battery compartments, keep batteries away from children

closureScript.diagnosis: Your daughter swallowed the button battery and it's stuck in her esophagus - the tube that goes from her mouth to her stomach. This is an emergency because these batteries create a chemical burn very quickly.
closureScript.plan: We need to take her to the operating room right now to remove the battery through her mouth using a scope. Every minute counts with this - the battery is burning her esophagus as we speak. The GI doctor is on the way.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: I have to be very direct - this is serious and time-critical. The sooner we get this out, the less damage there will be. There can be complications including scarring of her esophagus that might need future treatment.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You did exactly the right thing bringing her in immediately when you suspected she swallowed the battery. After this, we'll need to check all the remote controls and devices in your house - battery compartments need to be secured.

keyPoints: Heat stroke defined as core temperature >40°C (104°F) with CNS dysfunction

No evidence for dantrolene in exertional heat stroke

Check on vulnerable neighbors during heat waves
